examples.title: Forest Biomass Calculation Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to estimate forest carbon storage based on tree measurements and forest type coefficients.
examples.code:
BEGIN
 INPUT: tree_height (h), trunk_diameter (d), forest_type (f)
 
 // Volume calculation using allometric equation
 volume = 0.25 * PI * d^2 * h * form_factor
 
 // Forest type biomass coefficient (tons/m³)
 IF f = "tropical" THEN coefficient = 0.65
 ELSE IF f = "temperate" THEN coefficient = 0.50
 ELSE IF f = "boreal" THEN coefficient = 0.45
 END IF
 
 // Total biomass calculation
 biomass = volume * coefficient * area
 
 // Carbon content (approximately 50% of dry biomass)
 carbon_storage = biomass * 0.50
 
 // CO2 equivalent (carbon to CO2 conversion: 44/12)
 co2_equivalent = carbon_storage * (44/12)
 
 OUTPUT: carbon_storage, co2_equivalent
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm calculates forest carbon storage using basic tree measurements. The allometric equations estimate volume from diameter and height, then apply species-specific biomass coefficients. Carbon is approximately 50% of dry biomass, and converting to CO2 equivalent uses the molecular weight ratio 44/12.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) - fixed variables regardless of input size

examples.title: Forest Succession Simulation
examples.description: Simulates forest succession stages over time based on disturbance type and environmental conditions.
examples.code:
BEGIN
 INPUT: disturbance_type, soil_quality, climate, time_years
 
 FUNCTION succession_stage(time, disturbance):
 IF disturbance = "none" THEN
 RETURN "climax_forest"
 ELSE IF disturbance = "fire" AND soil_quality > 0.6 THEN
 // Secondary succession
 IF time < 5 THEN RETURN "pioneer_herbs"
 ELSE IF time < 20 THEN RETURN "shrubs_pioneer_trees"
 ELSE IF time < 100 THEN RETURN "young_forest"
 ELSE RETURN "mature_forest"
 ELSE IF disturbance = "volcanic" THEN
 // Primary succession
 IF time < 50 THEN RETURN "lichens_moss"
 ELSE IF time < 200 THEN RETURN "grasses_shrubs"
 ELSE IF time < 500 THEN RETURN "young_forest"
 ELSE RETURN "mature_forest"
 END IF
 RETURN "stable_state"
 
 FOR year FROM 1 TO time_years:
 current_stage = succession_stage(year, disturbance_type)
 OUTPUT "Year", year, ":", current_stage
 // Update soil through decomposition
 soil_quality = soil_quality + 0.01
 END FOR
END
examples.explanation: This simulation models forest succession by tracking different stages over time. Primary succession (on bare rock) takes centuries, while secondary succession (after fire with intact soil) progresses faster. The algorithm updates soil quality over time, simulating organic matter accumulation.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) - linear with number of years simulated
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) - constant memory for simulation variables

examples.title: Deforestation Risk Assessment Model
examples.description: Assesses deforestation risk based on proximity to roads, population density, and slope of terrain.
examples.code:
BEGIN
 INPUT: distance_to_road, population_density, slope_angle, protected_area
 
 // Initialize risk score
 risk_score = 0
 
 // Road proximity factor (closer = higher risk)
 IF distance_to_road < 1 THEN risk_score = risk_score + 40
 ELSE IF distance_to_road < 5 THEN risk_score = risk_score + 25
 ELSE IF distance_to_road < 20 THEN risk_score = risk_score + 10
 
 // Population density factor
 risk_score = risk_score + (population_density * 0.5)
 
 // Slope factor (flatter = easier deforestation)
 IF slope_angle < 10 THEN risk_score = risk_score + 20
 ELSE IF slope_angle < 25 THEN risk_score = risk_score + 10
 
 // Protected area reduction
 IF protected_area = TRUE THEN
 risk_score = risk_score * 0.2
 END IF
 
 // Risk classification
 IF risk_score > 70 THEN classification = "HIGH RISK"
 ELSE IF risk_score > 40 THEN classification = "MODERATE RISK"
 ELSE classification = "LOW RISK"
 
 OUTPUT: risk_score, classification
END
examples.explanation: This model assesses deforestation vulnerability using multiple factors. Roads provide access for logging and agriculture, population pressure increases demand for land, flatter terrain enables easier clearing, while protected areas significantly reduce risk. The weighted scoring helps prioritize conservation efforts.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) - constant time with fixed calculations
